museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: January 2014: The year begins with PS4 and Xbox One new but still thin on must-have games.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for January 2014. Regional timing and UK retail context should be checked before verification.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Dark Souls II
items.body: FromSoftware's formula becomes a recognised event.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 2014.

items.title: Titanfall
items.body: Respawn sells Xbox One momentum through wall-running mechs.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 2014.

items.title: Mario Kart 8
items.body: Nintendo gives Wii U a beautiful, badly needed system-seller.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for May 2014.

items.title: Watch Dogs
items.body: Ubisoft's long-hyped open world becomes a test of next-gen expectation.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for May 2014.

items.title: Shovel Knight
items.body: Yacht Club makes retro craft feel contemporary rather than merely nostalgic.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for June 2014.

items.title: Five Nights at Freddy's
items.body: A small horror game becomes a YouTube and lore phenomenon.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for August 2014.

items.title: Destiny
items.body: Bungie turns loot, strikes and social space into a platform promise.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for September 2014.

items.title: The Sims 4
items.body: Maxis restarts domestic routine on PC with a controversial feature gap.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for September 2014.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: PS4 and Xbox One settle in
items.body: The first full year is about proving value after launch hype.
items.sourceNote: 2014 platform context; verify sales claims before final copy.

items.title: Kinect is unbundled from Xbox One
items.body: Microsoft changes the Xbox One value argument by selling a cheaper Kinect-free model.
items.sourceNote: 2014 Xbox One context; verify exact regional dates before final copy.

items.title: Wii U gets key first-party support
items.body: Mario Kart 8, Bayonetta 2 and Smash give Nintendo loyalists a strong software year.
items.sourceNote: 2014 Wii U release context supports this reading.

items.title: Streaming becomes normalised
items.body: Twitch and console sharing make watching and broadcasting part of everyday game culture.
items.sourceNote: 2014 online/platform context; verify service milestones before verification.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Services and servers
items.body: Destiny makes weekly resets, raids and loot chatter part of the console routine.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Downloads and stores
items.body: Five Nights at Freddy's spreads through YouTube reactions and lore speculation.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Forums and feeds
items.body: Share buttons and Twitch make ordinary play more visible than before.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Everyday connection
items.body: Patches, delays, betas and server issues become part of the public life of a game.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like owning new hardware before the new era had fully arrived.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: There were brilliant games, but many of them were cross-gen, remastered, delayed or still finding their shape.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: Destiny made the future feel social and repetitive at once; Five Nights showed how fast YouTube could turn fear into folklore.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: By Christmas, the PS4 and Xbox One felt established, but their identities were still being negotiated in public.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
